coredata.dc:title: Biomass-derived carbon dots enhanced maize (Zea mays L.) drought tolerance by regulating phyllosphere microorganisms and ion fluxes

coredata.dc:description: Enhancing crop drought tolerance is beneficial for increasing crop yield and ensuring food security. This study demonstrates that foliar application of biomass-derived carbon dots (Soy-CDs, 10 mg·L−1) can enhance the drought tolerance of maize, leading to an increase in fresh weight of maize shoots and roots (50.5 % and 54.9 %) and dry weight of maize shoots and roots (40.0 % and 37.7 %), respectively. Meanwhile, Soy-CDs altered the abundance of phyllosphere microorganisms (e.g. Proteobacteria, Deinococcus-Thermus, Verrucomicrobia, and Actinobacteria) of maize under drought stress, which enhanced the nitrogen fixation and stress resistance in maize leaves. Besides, Soy-CDs also promote Ca2+ influx and Na+ efflux in maize mesophyll cells under drought stress, facilitating the regulation of ion osmotic balance and contributing to the improvement of maize drought tolerance. Furthermore, Soy-CDs enhanced photosynthesis (55.3 %) of maize under drought stress, and the full life cycle study showed that Soy-CDs can promote the metabolism of maize kernels, ultimately improve the yield (40.5 %) and nutritional quality of maize. Therefore, this work discovers the enormous potential of biomass-derived carbon dots in improving drought resistance and quality of crop, which contributes to ensure food security.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of Soy-CDs in different fields and size distribution of Soy-CDs (a); fluorescence spectra at 320–360 nm excitation of Soy-CDs (b); CLSM image of maize leaves after spraying Soy-CDs (c); 3D CLSM images of maize leaves after spraying Soy-CDs (d, e); TEM images of maize leaves under different visual fields after spraying Soy-CDs (f∼h).
Chord diagram of the top 10 phyla of bacteria (a); chord diagram of the top 5 phyla of fungi (b); heat map of the top 20 genera of bacteria (c); heat map of the top 20 genera of fungi (d).

Net Na+ flux (a); mean Na+ flux (b); net Ca2+ flux (c); mean Ca2+ flux (d) in maize mesophyll cells. Notes: the positive and negative signs of the flow rate values represent the direction of ion/molecule translocation only, with positive values indicating efflux from the leaf plastid to the leaf plastid exosome (cellular interstitium) and negative values indicating uptake from the leaf plastid exosome into the leaf plastid cell.

Photos of maize seedlings (a); fresh weight of maize shoots and roots (b); dry weight of maize shoots and roots (c); relative expression levels of ZmPIP1;1, ZmPIP1;2, ZmPIP2;4, ZmPIP2;5, ZmTIP1;1, and ZmNIP1;1 in maize roots (d); relative water content of maize shoots and roots (e); net photosynthetic rate of maize leaves (f); relative expression levels of ZmpsaK, Zmpsah1, Zmpsan2, and Zmpsb4 in maize leaves (g); the chlorophyll content (h); chlorophyll fluorescence parameters diagram of maize leaves (i).

The photographs of maize kernels (a); the yield per plant (b); protein content (c); 5 main fatty acid content in maize kernels (d); principal component analysis diagrams of maize kernels metabolic substances (e); classification of metabolic substances in maize kernels (f).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enhancing crop drought tolerance is beneficial for increasing crop yield and ensuring food security. This study demonstrates that foliar application of biomass-derived carbon dots (Soy-CDs, 10 mg·L−1) can enhance the drought tolerance of maize, leading to an increase in fresh weight of maize shoots and roots (50.5 % and 54.9 %) and dry weight of maize shoots and roots (40.0 % and 37.7 %), respectively. Meanwhile, Soy-CDs altered the abundance of phyllosphere microorganisms (e.g. Proteobacteria, Deinococcus-Thermus, Verrucomicrobia, and Actinobacteria) of maize under drought stress, which enhanced the nitrogen fixation and stress resistance in maize leaves. Besides, Soy-CDs also promote Ca2+ influx and Na+ efflux in maize mesophyll cells under drought stress, facilitating the regulation of ion osmotic balance and contributing to the improvement of maize drought tolerance. Furthermore, Soy-CDs enhanced photosynthesis (55.3 %) of maize under drought stress, and the full life cycle study showed that Soy-CDs can promote the metabolism of maize kernels, ultimately improve the yield (40.5 %) and nutritional quality of maize. Therefore, this work discovers the enormous potential of biomass-derived carbon dots in improving drought resistance and quality of crop, which contributes to ensure food security.

ce:section.ce:para: Drought, as a high-frequency natural disaster, has a significant impact on agriculture, water resources, environment, ecosystems, and human livelihoods. From 2006–2019, the average affected area of crops caused by drought was 7.526 million, with an annual loss of approximately 24.9 billion (¥) in economic crops and a loss of approximately 22 billion kilograms of food (Sun et al., 2023). Drought stress affected the morphological structure and physiological functions of plants, including roots, leaves, photosynthesis, osmotic regulation, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolism (Abid et al., 2018). Thus, improving the ability of crops to resist stress can promote crop growth, reduce yield losses, and is crucial for ensuring global food security (Santini et al., 2022).

Drought not only damages plants, but also involves the fungal and bacterial microbial communities associated with plants. Research has confirmed that crops and microorganisms (including fungi and bacteria) form important beneficial partnerships in different regions such as leaves, roots, and rhizosphere (Gao et al., 2022). The phyllosphere microorganisms come from soil, air, and seeds, which are mainly influenced by ecological and abiotic factors, and they perform various ecological functions including the homeostasis of leaf function and lifespan, seed quality, fruit development, and host growth (Xu et al., 2022). Chen et al. revealed that when important genetic elements such as basic immune pathways or regulating leaf water environment are missing in plants, bacteria in the leaves will proliferate excessively and the community structure will change (the proportion of different bacterial species will change and be imbalanced), leading to an imbalance in the microbial community, and the plant leaves will exhibit yellowing and necrosis phenotypes similar to diseases. Therefore, the balance of plant phyllosphere microorganisms is crucial for maintaining healthy plant growth (Chen et al., 2020). In other words, phyllosphere microorganisms can help plants resist pathogens, enhance tolerant stress, improve nutrient absorption, and regulate plant metabolic balance (Vogel et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2022b).

Moreover, when plants are subjected to drought stress, they perceive water deficiency signals through their leaves and roots. For instance, leaves control the opening and closing of stomata, and signal transduction of ABA, ROS, and Ca2+ (Liu et al., 2022). Stress can lead to excessive accumulation of Na+ in the cytoplasm, disrupting the ion balance within the cell, thereby damaging various basic cellular activities of plants and causing adverse effects on their growth (Chen et al., 2023). Accordingly, maintaining intracellular ion balance is also an important way for plants to respond to stress signals (Zhu et al., 2022a). Hence, regulating the ion balance of plant cells may be a new way to improve crop drought resistance.

Nanomaterials can be better absorbed by seeds/seedlings as initiators and trigger enhanced molecular interactions at the cellular level to enhance crop abiotic stress tolerance (M S et al., 2021). Research has found that nanoparticles can cause physiological effects in plants, including growth inhibition, increased antioxidant enzyme activity, changes in gene expression and metabolite composition, and even significant changes in the structure and composition of leaf microbial communities (Shi et al., 2024). Zhao et al. confirmed that nanomaterials can activate and regulate specific stress-related genes, which in turn increase the activity of heat shock protein and aquaporin to enable plants’ resistance against abiotic stresses (Zhao et al., 2023). Among them, biomass carbon dots (B-CDs), which are carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials synthesized from biomass, possess a wide range of sources and higher biocompatibility, demonstrating good effects in enhancing plant stress resistance and other aspects (Meng et al., 2019). For instance, Lei et al. demonstrated that miltiorrhiza-derived CDs (1.5 mg·mL−1) could trigger the Ca2+ pathway and promote environmental adaptation of plants under salinity and nutrient deficiency conditions (Li et al., 2022b). Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived CDs were effective regulators of Fe, Mn, Zn, and Cd accumulation in plants, thereby enhancing light signal transduction and triggering shoot-to-root deficiency response (Zhu et al., 2023b). Besides, Yang et al. confirmed that foliar application of CDs (5 mg·L−1) enhanced maize photosynthesis, thereby regulating the rhizosphere process and altering the abundance of rhizosphere microorganisms under drought stress (Yang et al., 2022). Soil application of CDs (5 mg·kg−1) could also recruite beneficial microbial communities in soybean rhizosphere under drought stress (Wang et al., 2022a). Zhu et al. found that foliar sprayed carbon dots can induce high affinity phosphorus absorption and transport systems in sweet potatoes, optimize root structure, improve the ability of sweet potatoes to absorb phosphorus, and maintain phosphorus homeostasis by regulating phosphorus metabolism processes in plants, thereby alleviating low phosphorus stress in sweet potatoes through transcriptome and metabolomics analysis (Zhu et al., 2024). Therefore, whether foliar application of biomass-derived CDs can alter the abundance of phyllosphere microorganisms and ion homeostasis in mesophyll cells, thereby enhancing plant drought resistance?

In this study, soybean was used as a carbon source to synthesize biomass-derived carbon dots (Soy-CDs), and maize was selected as the model crop to explore the effects of Soy-CDs on maize phyllosphere microorganisms and ion homeostasis in mesophyll cells under drought stress. Therefore, the following aspects of this study were explored: (1) the response of maize phyllospheric microorganisms to exposure to Soy-CDs under drought stress; (2) the regulation of Soy-CDs on ion flux in maize mesophyll cells; (3) the effects of Soy-CDs on drought tolerance, yield, and quality of maize.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Soy-CDs and cultivation of maize
ce:section.ce:para: Soybean powder (derived from soybean seeds through ground) was treated at 200℃ for 10 hours by hydrothermal synthesis to obtain Soy-CDs, and the specific synthesis method was described in text S1. The shape and size of Soy-CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM–2100, Hitachi, Japan), and the particle size distribution was analyzed by the Nano measurer software. The ζ potential of Soy-CDs was measured using a Zeta sizer (ZEN3600, Malvern, UK) (Wang et al., 2023). Fluorescence spectra of Soy-CDs were measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F–7000, Hitachi, Japan) (Cao et al., 2021a). The Soy-CDs in maize leaves were observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, A1, Nikon, JPN) and TEM (JEM–2100, Hitachi, Japan). The pre-processing method of maize leaves for CLSM was supplemented in text S2. The component and surface functional groups of Soy-CDs were analyzed by X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Kalpha, US) (Cao et al., 2021b). The element content in the material was analyzed by Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS, Hitachi Regulus8100, Japan).

Maize seeds (Zea mays L. No. 9 Suyu) were obtained from Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences. The soil was collected from Wuxi, China (31°29' N, 120°20' E), and the physical and chemical properties of the original soil were shown in the Table S1. The experiment was conducted in the greenhouse of Jiangnan University from April 2023 to August 2023, and the temperature and humidity for corn cultivation were natural conditions.

After 25 days of maize growth, drought conditions were initiated. The different treatments were performed as follows. Non-CK treatment: maize seedlings were grown under natural conditions with normal irrigation throughout the whole experiment, and the soil moisture was maintained about 75 %. CK and Soy-CDs treatment: 25-d-old maize seedlings were gradually exposed to drought stress by withholding irrigation. On day 28, each pot was weighed, and the soil moisture was maintained about 30 %. From day 29 to day 35, 5 mL of Soy-CDs (10 mg·L−1) were sprayed daily to the foliage of each maize plant and accordingly 5 mL of water was sprayed daily to the foliage of each maize plant as a control. Detailed maize cultivation methods were provided in text S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of physiological indicators of maize leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Before harvesting maize, photosynthetic rate, intracellular CO2 concentrations, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance were measured using a photosynthesis analyzer (PP Systems, TRGAS–1, China) on the fully expanded maize leaves. Total leaf chlorophyll content was measured on the youngest fully expanded leaf of each maize plant with a SPAD-502PLUS chlorophyll meter (Konika Minolta, Japan). Chlorophyll fluorescence parameters of maize leaves was measured in the dark using a chlorophyll fluorometer (Pocket PEA, Hansatech Instruments Ltd., UK). The total N content in maize leaves, maize roots, and soil were measured using an elemental analyzer (Elementar, Germany), and nitrogenase activity in maize leaves was determined using an acetylene reduction method. The relative moisture content in maize leaves was calculated as previously report (Chen et al., 2022). The specific measurement methods for SOD, POD, and CAT enzyme activities in maize leaves were elaborated in text S4. The content of proline in maize leaves was determined according to previous research (Pingle et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction and determination of maize phyllosphere microorganisms
ce:section.ce:para: The composition of phyllosphere microbiome was analyzed by high-throughput sequencing technology on an Illumina HiSeq 2500 instrument from personalbio (Personal Biotechnology Co., Ltd, Shanghai) primers. The extraction and determination of maize phyllosphere microorganisms was completed by personalbio. The primers used for bacterial sequencing were 799 F (5′- AACMGGATTAGATACCCKG-3′) and 1193 R (5′- ACGTCATCCCCACCTTCC-3′), and the primers used for fungal sequencing were ITS1F (5′-CTTGGTCATTTAGAGGAAGTAA-3′) and ITS2 (5′-GCTGCGTTCTTCATCGATGC-3′). After sequencing the V5-V7 and ITS1 (b) regions, the raw reads (original PE) were obtained, and low-quality fragments were filtered to recover valid tags.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of net Ca2+and Na+ fluxes in maize leaves
ce:section.ce:para: The net fluxes of Ca2+ and Na+ were measured using non-invasive micro-test technology (NMT, 100S-SIM-XY, Xuyue, Beijing, China) (Li et al., 2022b). Based on the previous research, microelectrodes were constructed to detect Ca2+ and Na+, respectively (Wang et al., 2022b). Pre-pulled and salinized microelectrodes (4.5 ± 0.5 μm, XY-CGQ-01) were filled with a backfilling solution (Ca2+: 100 mM CaCl2, pH = 6.0; Na+: 250 mM NaCl, pH = 6.0) to a length of about 1.0 cm followed by filling of selective liquid ion-exchange cocktails (LIXs, Ca2+: XY-SJ-H-10, 50 μm; Na+: XY-SJ-K-10, 50 μm; Younger, USA). Before measurement, teared the epidermis of maize leaves and added 5 mL of measuring solution (Ca2+: 0.1 mM CaCl2, pH = 6.0, time: 15 minutes; Na+: 0.25 mM NaCl, pH = 6.0, time: 6 hours) for equilibration. The calibration solution was comprised of the following: Ca2+, 0.05 and 0.5 mM CaCl2 (Nernstian slope 26 ± 5 mV per decade); Na+, 0.1 and 1.0 mM NaCl (Nernstian slope 58 ± 5 mV per decade), and the pH of calibration solution was adjusted to 6.0. Ion fluxes were measured at about 1–2 μm from the surface of maize mesophyll cells continuously for 6 minutes. All measurements were conducted at least 75 times. Three biological replicates from each treatment were measured. Finally, net Ca2+ and Na+ fluxes of maize mesophyll cells was calculated and analyzed using imFluxes V2.2 Software.

The relative expression levels of Zmpsak, Zmpsah1, Zmpsan2, ZmpsbA, and ZmAQPs in maize leaves were quantified with Ultra-SYBR mixture (CW Biotech Co., Ltd. Jiangsu, China) using a CFX96TM optics module real-time PCR detection system (Bio-Rad) (Wang et al., 2021). First, the total RNA of maize leaves was extracted using the RNA pure plant kit (DNaseI) (CW Biotech Co., Ltd. Jiangsu, China). Then the RNA was transcribed to cDNA by reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction with cDNA synthesis Kit (CW Biotech Co., Ltd. Jiangsu, China). The primers were designed and synthesized by the Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The specific analysis methods of relative gene expression levels and primers were listed in text S5 and Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of maize kernel yield and quality
ce:section.ce:para: When harvesting soybeans at maturity, the yield and 100-grain weight of maize kernels were measured immediately. The starch content of maize kernels was determined and analyzed using assay kits (Suzhou Grace Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China). The protein content of maize kernels was determined using a Kjeldahl automatic analyzer (K9840, Hanon). The fatty acid content of maize kernels was analyzed by gas chromatography (GC) (7890 A, Agilent, USA). The detailed measurements of maize kernels quality were supplemented in text S6. Referring to the previous method, the metabolite of maize kernels was extracted and determined by high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (Thermo Scientific, Germany) (Xiao et al., 2023). The specific extraction and determination of metabolites in maize kernels along with the setup and analysis of HPLC–MS/MS were supplemented in text S7.

The results of the measured parameters were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation of at least three biological replicates. The differences among treatments were analyzed with one way analysis of variances (ANOVA) followed by Tukey’s post-hoc test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Soy-CDs and their distribution in maize leaves
ce:section.ce:para: As prepared Soy-CDs were spherical particles and good dispersion, with an average diameter of 6.5 ± 0.7 nm ( Fig. 1a), and the ζ potential of Soy-CDs was −24.1 ± 1.8 mV, which indicated that Soy-CDs were negatively charged (Fig. S1a). Studies demonstrated that such small-sized and negatively-charged CDs can enter plant leaves, disperse into the cytoplasm and chloroplasts, and evenly distribute within the leaves. Besides, Soy-CDs showed an emission maximum at 436 nm upon excitation at 320 nm (Fig. 1b), which indicated that Soy-CDs can absorb ultraviolet light and emits blue light. XPS results clearly confirmed the presence of C (284.8 eV), N (400.1 eV), and O (532.1 eV) on the surface of Soy-CDs (Fig. S1b) (Yao et al., 2022). The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra showed the presence of C–C, C–O, C–N, and CO in Soy-CDs (Fig. S1c) (Li et al., 2020). The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra showed the presence of pyridine N and pyrrolic N in Soy-CDs (Fig. S1d) (Li et al., 2021a). The high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectra showed the presence of O–H and lattice oxygen in Soy-CDs (Fig. S1e) (Li et al., 2020). These results confirmed the abundant functional groups and chemical composition on the surface of Soy-CDs, indicating that Soy-CDs have excellent water-dispersible and biocompatibility (Khan et al., 2022), which facilitates the entry of small-sized carbon dots into plant cells and thus their function.

CLSM image and 3D CLSM images clearly showed that Soy-CDs can enter plant leaves and emit strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 1c-e). Furthermore, compared with CK and Non-CK (Fig. S2a-f), TEM images exhibited the aggregation and distribution of Soy-CDs in maize leaves, implying that Soy-CDs could penetrate the stomata and vein of maize leaves and disperse around chloroplasts (Fig. 1f-h). The EDS spectrum shows the presence of C, O, N, Ca, and Mg, indicating that the Soy-CDs contain trace amounts of Ca and Mg elements (Fig. S3), which is similar to our previous report (Cheng et al., 2023). Overall, small-sized CDs made from soybean possessed hydrophilic functional groups and were able to enter maize leaves, and were translocated to maize chloroplasts.

Climate change such as drought can lead to instability of plant leaf microbial communities, and the reduction of beneficial groups can weaken the plant's resistance to pathogen invasion (Zhu et al., 2022c). β-diversity mainly reflects the variability of different species under different treatments (Mori et al., 2018). In Fig. S4a and Fig. S4c, the composition of bacterial communities (PC1: 37.1 %) and fungal communities (PC1: 88.3 %) in maize leaves among the three treatment groups (Non-CK, CK, and Soy-CDs) was relatively similar. α-diversity reflects the abundance and diversity of microbial communities. The results of Chao1, obsearved_species, and Shannon indicated no significant difference in the richness and diversity of bacteria and fungi in maize leaves (Fig. S4b and Fig. S4d), implying that drought stress and Soy-CDs application hardly changed the community abundance of phyllosphere microorganism.

Under drought stress, the abundance of Proteobacteria, Deinococcus-Thermus, Verrucomicrobia, and Patescibacteria significantly decreased in the phylum bacterium, but increased by 16.14 %, 244.44 %, 15.00 %, and 211.11 %, respectively, after spraying Soy-CDs. The abundance of Bacteroidetes, Actinobacteria, Epsilonbacteraeota, and Tenericutes increased in the phylum bacterium under drought stress, while decreased by 12.40 %, 3.89 %, 28.30 %, and 52.38 %, respectively after spraying Soy-CDs ( Fig. 2a and Table S3). Meanwhile, Soy-CDs decreased the abundance of dominant Ascomycota phyla by 2.85 %, and increased the abundance of dominant Basidiomycota phyla by 98.38 % under drought stress. (Fig. 2b and Table S3).

Among them, Proteobacteria are more sensitive to environmental changes because they contain large numbers of non-dormant cells, resulting in increased or decreased abundance (Fan et al., 2023). The nitrogen fixing bacteria in the leaves are mainly composed of γ-Proteobacteria and Cyanobacteria. Herein, the increase of Proteobacteria may improve the nitrogen fixation ability of leaves, and phyllosphere microorganisms may enhance the plant's ability to adapt to the environment through nitrogen fixation (Zhu et al., 2023a). The Deinococcus-Thermus phylum includes some spherical bacteria that can resist harsh environments (Ho et al., 2016). The Verrucomicrobia phylum can obtain energy through chemoautotrophic metabolism under oligotrophic conditions (Tran et al., 2018). The vast majority of Actinobacteria are saprophytic, which may cause plant decay (Bhatti et al., 2017). Therefore, Soy-CDs increased the abundance of beneficial microorganisms in maize leaves under drought stress and reduced the abundance of pathogenic bacteria, which plays an important role in enhancing leaf resistance to drought stress.

Additionally, the application of Soy-CDs did not significantly alter the abundance of genus bacteria and fungi. Wherein, Soy-CDs increased the abundance of Sphingomonas and Paracoccus by 5.6- and 14-fold under drought conditions (Fig. 2b and Table S4). Sphingomonas possess efficient metabolic regulation mechanisms and gene regulation abilities, and have enormous potential in promoting plant growth and other aspects (Li et al., 2022a). Paracoccus is an aerobic or facultative anaerobic non-fermentative gram-negative bacteria, which can utilize NO3 – as a nitrogen nutrient and can reduce NO3 – to NO and N2 under anaerobic conditions, and it is also an ideal group for exploring environmentally friendly metabolic functions and environmental purification (Kelly et al., 2006; Puri et al., 2022). Similarly, Soy-CDs down-regulated the abundance of Byssochlamys by 44.1 % under drought stress, which belongs to Ascomycota (Fig. 2d and Table S4). The nutritional types of Ascomycota are mostly saprophytic fungi, and a decrease in their abundance may be beneficial for protecting plant growth and enhancing plant stress resistance (Challacombe et al., 2019).

As mentioned earlier, the changes in these microorganisms may enhance the nitrogen fixation ability and stress resistance of maize leaves. For example, Nitrogen-fixing bacteria in leaves consist mainly of γ-proteobacteria and cyanobacteria, and an increase in these bacteria increases the photosynthetic and nitrogen-fixing capacity of leaves (Zhu et al., 2023a). Our study confirmed that under drought stress, the application of Soy-CDs elevated the nitrogenase activity in maize shoots by 3.0 % (Fig. S5a), while increasing N content in soybean shoots and roots by 17.7 % and 46.7 %, respectively (Fig. S5b and c). Coincidentally, the N content of the soil was reduced by 8.1 % following the application of Soy-CDs under drought stress (Fig. S5d), perhaps attributable to Soy-CDs promoting N uptake by maize. Therefore, it may be the joint action of nitrogen-fixing microorganisms and Soy-CDs that increases the N content in maize leaves. Nitrogen in leaves is a component of chlorophyll and also a component of many enzymes in crops, playing a crucial role in plant growth, development, metabolism, and resistance of maize (Wang et al., 2014). In summary, Soy-CDs altered the abundance of dominant bacteria and decreased the abundance of saprophytic fungi in maize leaves under drought stress, thereby enhancing the uptake and utilization of N by leaves and strengthening the ability of leaves to resist stress, which is conducive to the enhancement of drought tolerance in maize.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soy-CDs regulated Na+ and Ca2+ fluxes in maize mesophyll cells
ce:section.ce:para: The ion balance of plants is severely disrupted under stress, especially the fluxes of Na+ and Ca2+. Under stressful conditions, plants are subjected to physiological influences that can lead to osmotic stress and excessive accumulation of Na+ in the cytoplasm (dos Santos et al., 2022). For instance, in high-salt environments, plants reduce intracellular Na+ concentration through strategies such as Na+ efflux or intracellular compartmentalization, which in turn re-establishes or maintains ionic homeostatic balance in the plant (Malakar and Chattopadhyay, 2021). Li et al. discovered a new CBL kinase HbCIPK2 that prevents K+ loss in the roots of Arabidopsis and reduces Na+ accumulation in the stems, maintaining K+/Na+ balance and protecting root cells to improve Arabidopsis's salt and drought resistance (Li et al., 2012). Besides, Ca2+ are also essential intermediate elements for plant growth and are also known as the "second messenger" (Tian et al., 2020). Plant cells can use intracellular Ca2+ as a mediator to respond to external environmental changes (Zhu, 2016). Previous studies have demonstrated that drought stress affects barley leaf ABA concentration and calcium ion flow rate in chloroplasts (Li et al., 2021b). Ca2+ efflux may compromise membrane integrity but can trigger ABA signaling that enhances plant acclimation to drought stress by increasing Ca2+ availability in the plastid ectodomain (Mak et al., 2014). Therefore, maintaining ion homeostasis is an important aspect of improving plant tolerance (Guo et al., 2020).

In this study, we used NMT to measure the flow rates of Na+ and Ca2+ in maize mesophyll cells (Fig. S6a and b). It was obvious that under non-drought stress, Na+ in maize mesophyll cells showed an outflow state with an average flow rate of 532.7 pmol cm−2 s−1. Under drought stress, Na+ in maize mesophyll cells showed a significant inward flow trend with an average flow rate of 266.4 pmol cm−2 s−1 ( Fig. 3a). This may be because under stress conditions, Na+ enters cells through non selective cation channels and exhibits an influx state (Khan et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2016). An increase in Na+ influx into plant leaves can cause imbalanced osmotic pressure both inside and outside the cell, leading to poor cell growth (Keisham et al., 2018). However, compared with CK, Na+ in maize mesophyll cells transitioned into an outflow trend after spraying Soy-CDs, with an average flow rate of 298.7 pmol cm−2 s−1 (Fig. 3b). Previous studies have reported that PNC (poly acrylic acid coated nanoceria) maintained sodium-potassium homeostasis by promoting leaf Na+ efflux and K+ retention, rather than Na+ vesicle storage, thereby improving cotton salt tolerance (Liu et al., 2021). Therefore, we hypothesized that foliar application of Soy-CDs promoted Na+ efflux from corn leaves, which was conducive to maintaining ionic homeostasis.

Meanwhile, Ca2+ in maize mesophyll cells showed an outflow state under non-drought stress, with an average flow rate of 27.6 pmol cm−2 s−1. When subjected to drought stress, Ca2+ shifted to an inward flow trend under drought stress with an average flow rate of −32.7 pmol cm−2 s−1 (Fig. 3c). Knight et al. found that Ca2+ channel proteins controlled by stress exist on plant cell membranes, which can quickly respond to stress, leading to extracellular Ca2+ influx and a series of signal transduction reactions (Knight et al., 1997). However, Soy-CDs promoted Ca2+ influx at an average flow rate of −73.7 pmol cm−2 s−1, an increase of 125.2 % compared to CK (Fig. 3d). Li et al. confirmed that Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived CDs trigger the hyperpolarization of membrane potential (MP) and Ca2+ influx in sweet potato roots, partially dependent on the -OH and COOH groups on its surface, which favors a positive regulatory effect on the plant's adaptation to environmental stress (Li et al., 2022b). Interestingly, Soy-CDs also promote Ca2+ influx into maize mesophyll cells, but it is not yet clear how they affect the Ca2+ signaling pathway. Thus, Soy-CDs may activate Ca2+ channels on the lipid membrane and promote Ca2+ influx into cells, while maintaining Na+ balance in mesophyll cells under drought stress, which might be beneficial for improving the ionic homeostasis of maize maize mesophyll cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soy-CDs improved photosynthesis and water absorption of maize
ce:section.ce:para: The growth of maize was significantly improved though treatment with Soy-CDs under drought stress ( Fig. 4a). The application of Soy-CDs increased the fresh weight of maize shoots and roots by 50.5 % and 54.9 % (Fig. 4b), and the dry weight of maize shoots and roots by 40.0 % and 37.7 % under drought stress (Fig. 4c). Drought is an important factor limiting plant production, and a well-developed root system helps plants absorb more water and nutrients from the soil (Gao et al., 2023). Notably, Soy-CDs improved the morphology of maize roots under drought stress (Fig. S7a), as evidenced by a 44.6 % increase in root length, a 34.8 % increase in root average diameter, a 31.2 % increase in root surface area, and a 43.0 % increase in root tips, respectively (Fig. S7b∼e). Meanwhile, Soy-CDs promoted the activity of aquaporins (AQPs) in maize roots under drought stress (Afzal et al., 2016). Compared with CK, the expression levels of ZmPIP1;1, ZmPIP1;2, ZmPIP2;4, ZmPIP2;5, ZmTIP1;1, and ZmNIP1;1 genes were up-regulated by 1.7-, 1.9-, 1.2-, 1.2-, 1.3-, and 1.3-fold, respectively, after foliar applying of Soy-CDs (Fig. 4d). The PIPs mainly control the transport of molecules on water channel protein membranes, TIPs mainly mediate intracellular or intercellular water transport across membranes, and NIPs are membrane proteins that transport water molecules and some small molecules on the cell membrane, these genes playing important roles in plant growth and resistance to stress (Kapilan et al., 2018). As a result, Soy-CDs increased the relative water content of maize shoots and roots by 1.0 % and 2.0 % under drought stress (Fig. 4e), indicating Soy-CDs promoted water absorption in maize roots by up-regulating the expression of AQPs genes and improving root growth.

In this study, Soy-CDs improved the photosynthesis of maize under drought stress. Compared with CK, the application of Soy-CDs improved the net photosynthetic rate (Pn), intercellular CO2 concentration (Ci), transpiration rate (Tr), and stomatal conductance (Gs) of maize leaves increased by 55.3 %, 40.0 %, 53.1 %, and 41.3 %, respectively (Fig. 4f and Fig. S8a∼c). Besides, the expression levels of ZmpsaK (mainly adjusting the expression of green tissue in photosystem Ⅰ), Zmpsah1 (mainly enhancing plant stress resistance), Zmpsan2 (adjusting electron transfer in photosystem Ⅰ), and ZmpsbA genes (mainly regulating the antioxidant system) demonstrated a 1.9-, 1.8-, 1.5-, and 1.7-fold up-regulation, respectively (Fig. 4g). Under drought stress, Soy-CDs increased the chlorophyll content of maize leaves increased by 8.9 % (Fig. 4h). The increase of chlorophyll can maintain the growth and development of the plant. Compared with CK, chlorophyll fluorescence parameters (such as Fv/Fm, qL, Rfd, etc.) in maize leaves were decreased under drought stress, but increased after spraying Soy-CDs (Fig. 4i and Table S5). These results indicated that Soy-CDs could alter the state of chlorophyll and the rate of photosynthesis, which is beneficial for maintaining the growth of maize under drought stress. Furthermore, CDs also have ROS scavenging properties that protect plants from stress-induced oxidative damage (Li et al., 2022b). The activities of SOD, POD, CAT enzyme in maize leaves increased under drought stress, but decreased by 34.8 %, 29.4 %, and 34.3 %, respectively, after foliar spraying of Soy-CDs (Fig. S9a∼c). Similarly, the proline content in maize leaves increased under drought stress, but decreased by 32.9 % after foliar spraying of Soy-CDs (Fig. S9d).

Overall, foliar application of Soy-CDs can enter maize leaves, enhance photosystem activity and promote chlorophyll synthesis by up-regulating the expression of photosystem genes, thereby protecting its photosynthetic system and antioxidant enzyme system under drought conditions. Therefore, the growth and the drought resistance of maize were improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soy-CDs increased the yield and quality of maize kernels
ce:section.ce:para: Soy-CDs improved the photosynthesis and drought tolerance of maize under drought stress, which is beneficial for the accumulation of photosynthetic products and thus may increase maize yield and quality. The full life study confirmed that Soy-CDs improved the yield and quality of maize kernels under drought stress ( Fig. 5a). Compared with CK, the yield per plant and the 100-grain weight of maize seeds increased by 40.5 % and 25.3 % after spraying Soy-CDs (Fig. 5b and S10a). Besides, under drought stress, the protein content and the starch content of maize kernels increased by 10.5 % and 8.0 % after spraying Soy-CDs (Fig. 5c and S10b). Carbohydrates can be synthesized into pyruvate through complex transformations, thereby participating in the TCA cycle and generating α- Ketoglutaric acid, in turn, participates in the synthesis of amino acids and promotes the accumulation of proteins in kernels (Zhao et al., 2019). In the present study, the content of 5 main fatty acids including linoleic acid, oleic acid, palmitic acid, stearic acid, and α-linolenic acid increased by 10.5 %, 8.8 %, 11.8 %, 12.3 %, and 12.3 %, respectively, after spraying Soy-CDs (Fig. 5d). Plant fatty acids are an important component of fats and phospholipids, serving as important energy substances and signaling molecules (Ye et al., 2020).

Furthermore, a total of 32 differential metabolites were screened for the metabolic substances in maize kernels using non-target metabolomics (Fig. S11 and Table S6). The principal component analysis (PCA) diagram revealed the differences among Non-CK, CK, and Soy-CDs (Fig. 5e). In general, the metabolites of maize kernels were mainly classified into several categories: fatty acids and conjugates (n=14), alcohols and polyols (n=4), organic acids and derivatives (n=3), terpenoids (n=3), ketone compounds (n=2), sugars (n=1), amino acids (n=1), nucleosides (n=1), and other substances (n=3) (Table S6). The application of Soy-CDs under drought stress can promote the synthesis of fatty acids and conjugates, organic acids and derivatives, sugars, amino acids, alcohols and polyols, ketone compounds in maize kernels, increasing by 19.3 %, 7.3 %, 19.8 %, 40.9 %, 94.8 %, and 33.2 %, respectively (Table S6). The result demonstrated that Soy-CDs can regulate the content of compounds (such as fatty acids, sugars, amino acids, and starch) by regulating the pathway of substance synthesis and metabolite synthesis in maize kernels under drought stress. Overall, Soy-CDs enhanced the photosynthesis and promoted the growth of maize under drought stress, which could promote the accumulation of photosynthetic products (such as carbohydrates), and facilitate the transport of carbohydrates from the source (leaves) to the sink (fruits) (Swift et al., 2021). Therefore, ultimately improved the yield and quality of maize kernels, indicating that Soy-CDs has great potential for application in crop production.

This study confirmed that foliar spraying of Soy-CDs (10 mg·L−1) enhanced maize drought tolerance by regulating phyllosphere microbial abundance and ion homeostasis in mesophyll cells. Soy-CDs can penetrate the cell wall of maize leaves and disperse around chloroplasts, thereby improving maize photosynthesis. Soy-CDs increased the abundance of beneficial microorganisms in maize leaves under drought stress and reduced the abundance of pathogenic bacteria, which plays an important role in enhancing leaf resistance to drought stress. Soy-CDs promoted photosynthesis in maize leaves, increased biomass accumulation, protected the antioxidant enzyme system, and also up-regulated the expression of root water channel proteins to promote root water uptake. The full life cycle experiment indicated that Soy-CDs enhanced drought tolerance of maize, and increased metabolites such as fatty acids, sugars, amino acids, and starch in kernels, ultimately improving maize yield and quality. Overall, this study investigated the regulatory role of biomass-derived CDs in plant tolerance and provided strategies for agriculture production under stress conditions.
